chunks.content: Any BSc CS student can choose to continue to the fourth year Honours programme. There are no eligibility criteria for the fourth year. Students who do not wish to continue can exit after Semester 6 with a three-year UG degree. Within the fourth year, students can choose Honours with Research only if they have scored 75% marks or above, equivalently CGPA 7.5 and above, cumulatively in the first six semesters. Regular Honours and Honours with Project are open to all fourth year students without any grade condition.

Calicut University uses a 10-point indirect grading system. The grades are: O (Outstanding) for 95% and above, grade point 10. A+ (Excellent) for above 85% and below 95%, grade point 9. A (Very Good) for 75% to below 85%, grade point 8. B+ (Good) for 65% to below 75%, grade point 7. B (Above Average) for 55% to below 65%, grade point 6. C (Average) for 45% to below 55%, grade point 5. P (Pass) for 35% to below 45% aggregate with minimum 30% in external evaluation, grade point 4. F (Fail) for below 35% aggregate or below 30% in external evaluation, grade point 0. Ab (Absent) is treated as Fail with grade point 0. First Class with Distinction requires grade point 8.50 to 10. First Class requires 5.50 to 8.49. Second Class requires 4.50 to 5.49. Third Class requires 3.50 to 4.49.

SGPA (Semester Grade Point Average) is calculated by multiplying the credits of each course by the grade points scored, summing all those values, then dividing by the total credits in that semester. For example if a student takes 6 courses with credits 3, 4, 3, 3, 3, 4 and scores grade points 8, 7, 6, 10, 5, 6 respectively, the total credit points are 24+28+18+30+15+24 = 139 and total credits are 20, so SGPA is 139 divided by 20 which equals 6.950. CGPA (Cumulative Grade Point Average) is calculated at the end of the programme using all courses taken. For the three year programme CGPA is based on all courses in semesters 1 to 6. For the four year programme CGPA includes all courses in semesters 1 to 8. SGPA and CGPA are both rounded to three decimal points and reported in transcripts. The minimum requirement to pass is P grade in all courses.

chunks.topic: Evaluation Scheme for Major and Minor Courses
chunks.content: Each course has two parts of evaluation: internal (30%) and external (70%). For 4-credit Major and Minor courses the total marks are 100, with 30 marks internal and 70 marks external. For 3-credit General Foundation courses the total marks are 75, with 25 marks internal and 50 marks external. There are two types of 4-credit courses: theory only courses and courses with 3-credit theory and 1-credit practical. In theory only courses, out of 5 modules one open-ended module worth 20% content is designed by the faculty and evaluated internally for 10 marks, and the remaining 4 theory modules are evaluated internally for 20 marks, totaling 30 internal marks. In courses with practical component, 4 modules are theory and the fifth module is practical. The practical is internally evaluated for 20 marks and the 4 theory modules are internally evaluated for 10 marks, totaling 30 internal marks. External evaluation carries 70 marks and examinations are conducted at the end of each semester.

chunks.topic: Internal Evaluation Components for Major and Minor Courses
chunks.content: For 4-credit Major and Minor theory only courses, internal evaluation of 30 marks is split as follows: Test paper or Mid-semester Exam carries 10 marks for theory modules and 4 marks for open-ended module totaling 14 marks. Seminar, Viva, or Quiz carries 6 marks for theory and 4 marks for open-ended totaling 10 marks. Assignment carries 4 marks for theory and 2 marks for open-ended totaling 6 marks. For 4-credit courses with practical component, internal evaluation is split as: Test paper or Mid-semester Exam carries 5 marks for theory modules. Seminar, Viva, or Quiz carries 3 marks. Assignment carries 2 marks. Practical component carries 20 marks evaluated separately. For 3-credit General Foundation courses internal evaluation of 25 marks is split as: Test paper or Mid-semester Exam carries 10 marks for theory and 2 marks for open-ended module. Seminar, Viva, or Quiz carries 6 marks for theory and 2 marks for open-ended. Assignment carries 4 marks for theory and 1 mark for open-ended totaling 25 marks.

chunks.topic: Practical Component Evaluation for Major and Minor Courses
chunks.content: The practical component in Major and Minor courses is completely evaluated internally. Total practical marks are 20. Continuous evaluation of practical exercises performed in practical classes carries 10 marks with 50% weightage. End-semester practical examination and viva-voce conducted by the teacher-in-charge along with an internal examiner appointed by the Department Council carries 7 marks with 35% weightage. Evaluation of practical records submitted for the end semester viva voce examination carries 3 marks with 15% weightage. The process of continuous evaluation must be completed before 10 days from the commencement of the end-semester examination. Only students who passed in continuous evaluation are permitted to appear for the end-semester practical examination and viva-voce.

For Major and Minor courses the external examination is 2 hours duration for 70 marks. The question paper has three sections. Short Answer questions: 10 questions are given, 8 must be answered, each carries 3 marks, ceiling is 24 marks. Paragraph or Problem questions: 8 questions are given, 6 must be answered, each carries 6 marks, ceiling is 36 marks. Essay questions: 2 questions are given, 1 must be answered, carries 10 marks. Total external marks are 70. For General Foundation courses the external examination is 1.5 hours duration for 50 marks. Short Answer questions: 10 questions are given, 8 must be answered, each carries 2 marks, ceiling is 16 marks. Paragraph or Problem questions: 5 questions are given, 4 must be answered, each carries 6 marks, ceiling is 24 marks. Essay questions: 2 questions are given, 1 must be answered, carries 10 marks. Total external marks are 50.

All students must undergo an internship of 2 credits during the first six semesters in a firm, industry, organization, or training in labs with faculty and researchers of their own institution or other Higher Educational Institutions or research institutions. The internship can be in Computer Science or allied disciplines. There should be minimum 60 hours of engagement from the student. Summer vacations and other holidays can be used for completing the internship. A faculty member or scientist or instructor of the respective institution where the student does the internship should be the supervisor. The institution must be prior-approved by the Department Council. Students must maintain a personal log book with regular detailed entries throughout the internship period. The log book and typed report must be submitted at the end. A visit to minimum one national research institute or research laboratory or place of scientific importance should be part of the internship as a study tour and a brief report with photos must be submitted. The credits and marks for internship are awarded only at the end of Semester 6. Total internship marks are 50. Continuous evaluation carries 20 marks including skill acquisition, interim presentation and viva-voce, punctuality and log book. Report of institute visit carries 5 marks. End-semester viva-voce carries 17 marks including quality of work, presentation, and viva-voce. Evaluation of records and final report carries 8 marks.

chunks.topic: Project Requirements for Honours and Honours with Research
chunks.content: In the Honours programme students have the option to do a Project of 12 credits instead of three Core Courses in Major in Semester 8. The project can be done in the same institution or any other higher educational institution or research center. A faculty member of the respective institution where the student does the project should be the supervisor. The project must be prior-approved by the Department Council including the institution and supervisor. Project can be in Computer Science or allied disciplines and must be done individually. Project work can be experimental, theoretical, or computational in nature. There should be minimum 240 hours of engagement for Honours programme project. For Honours with Research programme the project is mandatory with minimum 360 hours of engagement. Students must maintain a personal log book throughout the project period. The log book and typed report must be submitted at the end and a soft copy must also be submitted. It is desirable but not mandatory to publish results in a peer reviewed journal. Project is evaluated for 300 marks total. Internal evaluation carries 90 marks split as: skill in doing project work 30 marks, interim presentation and viva-voce 20 marks, punctuality and log book 20 marks, scheme and organization of project report 20 marks. External evaluation carries 210 marks split as: content, relevance, methodology, quality of analysis and innovations 50 marks, presentation of project 50 marks, project report and log book and references 60 marks, viva-voce 50 marks.

Students who score 75% marks and above, equivalently CGPA 7.5 and above, cumulatively in the first six semesters are eligible for Honours with Research stream in the fourth year. In Honours with Research the student must do a mandatory Research Project of 12 credits in Semester 8. The approved research centres of University of Calicut or any other university or Higher Educational Institution can offer the Honours with Research programme. Departments in affiliated colleges under Calicut University that are not approved research centres must get prior approval from the University to offer Honours with Research. Such departments must have minimum one faculty member with PhD degree and must also have the necessary infrastructure. A faculty member with a PhD degree can supervise the research project of Honours with Research students. One faculty member can supervise maximum four students in Honours with Research stream. The research project requires minimum 360 hours of engagement compared to 240 hours for regular Honours project. Students in Honours with Research also have the option to take Research Methodology as a course coded CSC8CJ in Semester 8 instead of one elective course.

chunks.topic: Credit Distribution for Three Year and Four Year Programme
chunks.content: For the three year programme the total credits are 133 and total marks are 3325. Semester 1 has 21 credits. Semester 2 has 21 credits. Semester 3 has 22 credits. Semester 4 has 21 credits. Semester 5 has 23 credits. Semester 6 has 25 credits including 2 credits for internship. For the four year programme the total credits are 177 and total marks are 4425. Semester 7 has 20 credits. Semester 8 has 24 credits including 12 credits for project in Honours or Honours with Research programme. Credit distribution for three years is: Major courses 68 credits, Minor courses 24 credits, General Foundation courses 39 credits, Internship 2 credits, totaling 133 credits. Credit distribution for four years is: Major courses 100 credits including 12 project credits, Minor courses 36 credits, General Foundation courses 39 credits, Internship 2 credits, totaling 177 credits. Each Major and Minor course carries 4 credits. Each General Foundation course carries 3 credits. Internship carries 2 credits. Project carries 12 credits.

chunks.topic: SEC 3 Mini Project Implementation in Semester 6
chunks.content: SEC 3 in Semester 6 is a mandatory mini-project worth 3 credits, designed to develop students' research and software development skills as a capstone experience bridging classroom theory and real-world application. Students form groups of at most four members and propose their own project topic in Computer Science or related disciplines. Projects can be experimental (building a prototype), theoretical (a research paper), or computational (implementing an algorithm). Project proposals must be submitted for prior approval from the Department Council, and each project team is assigned a project supervisor for guidance. The mini-project duration is one semester with a minimum engagement of 90 hours per student. Deliverables include two hard copies and one softcopy of a structured report covering objectives, requirements analysis, system design, implementation details, test cases, results, and conclusion, along with a signed originality undertaking and a supervisor certificate. Evaluation totals 75 marks: Internal evaluation by the project supervisor is 25 marks, split as Project Proposal and Planning 5 marks, Project Progress and Implementation 10 marks, and Interim Presentations 10 marks. External evaluation is 50 marks, conducted by an external examiner, covering the project report quality, project demonstration, and viva-voce on project concepts and critical thinking. This SEC 3 mini-project in Semester 6 is distinct from the larger 12-credit Honours Project or Honours with Research project that students may undertake in Semester 8 of the fourth year.

Students choose elective courses in Semesters 5, 6, and 8. In Semesters 5 and 6 students choose two elective courses each semester from a basket of electives with specialization. There are three specialization groups to choose from. Group 1 is Data Science which includes Mathematical and Statistical Foundations for Data Science in Semester 5, Exploratory Data Analysis in Semester 5, Introduction to Data Warehousing and Big Data in Semester 6, and Advanced Python for Data Science in Semester 6. Group 2 is AI and ML which includes Machine Learning Algorithms in Semester 5, Knowledge Engineering in Semester 5, Soft Computing in Semester 6, and Deep Learning in Semester 6. Group 3 is Cloud Computing which includes Cloud Computing in Semester 5, Security and Privacy in Cloud in Semester 5, Storage Technologies in Semester 6, and Virtualization in Semester 6. Students must choose two elective courses from the same specialization group in Semester 5 and two from the same group in Semester 6. In Semester 8 students choose three elective courses from a basket with no specialization restriction. The Semester 8 electives without specialization include Microprocessor and its Applications, System Software, Social Network Analysis, Advanced Distributed Computing, Cyber Forensic, Ethical Hacking, and Expert Systems.

Semester 1 Major Course: CSC1CJ101 - Fundamentals of Computers and Computational Thinking (75 hours, 4 credits). Semester 2 Major Course: CSC2CJ101 - Fundamentals of Programming in C Language (75 hours, 4 credits). Semester 3 Major Courses: CSC3CJ201 - Software Project Management (60 hours, 4 credits) and CSC3CJ202 - Data Structures and Algorithm (75 hours, 4 credits). Semester 4 Major Courses: CSC4CJ203 - Database Management System (75 hours, 4 credits) and CSC4CJ204 - Fundamentals of Python Programming (75 hours, 4 credits) and CSC4CJ205 - Computer Networks (75 hours, 4 credits). Semester 5 Major Courses: CSC5CJ301 - Data Mining (60 hours, 4 credits) and CSC5CJ302 - Object Oriented Programming Java (75 hours, 4 credits) and CSC5CJ303 - Full Stack Web Development (75 hours, 4 credits). Semester 6 Major Courses: CSC6CJ304 - Digital Electronics and Computer Architecture (60 hours, 4 credits) and CSC6CJ305 - Principles of Operating Systems (75 hours, 4 credits) and CSC6CJ306 - Introduction to Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning (75 hours, 4 credits). Semester 7 Major Courses: CSC7CJ401 - Theory of Computation (60 hours, 4 credits) and CSC7CJ402 - System Security (60 hours, 4 credits) and CSC7CJ403 - Advanced Data Structures and Algorithms (75 hours, 4 credits) and CSC7CJ404 - Blockchain Technology (60 hours, 4 credits) and CSC7CJ405 - Internet of Things (75 hours, 4 credits). Semester 8 Major Courses for Regular Honours: CSC8CJ406 - Compiler Design (60 hours, 4 credits) and CSC8CJ407 - Client Server Architecture (60 hours, 4 credits) and CSC8CJ408 - Parallel Computing (60 hours, 4 credits).

chunks.topic: General Foundation Courses AEC VAC SEC and MDC
chunks.content: General Foundation courses are 3-credit courses evaluated for 75 marks with 25 internal and 50 external marks. CS students take the following fixed General Foundation courses: AEC Semester 1 is ENG1FA101 English Language Skills for Sciences (60 hours). AEC Semester 2 is ENG2FA103 Advanced English Language Skills for Sciences (60 hours). VAC Semester 3 is ENG3FV108 Framing Values Literary and Cultural Narratives for Sciences (45 hours). VAC Semester 4 is ENG4FV109 Framing Perspectives Literary and Cultural Narratives for Sciences (45 hours). SEC Semester 4 is ENG4FS111 Contemporary Language Skills for Sciences (60 hours). SEC Semester 5 is Introduction to Digital Marketing (45 hours). SEC Semester 6 is Project Implementation (45 hours). Additionally each student takes a Language course of their choice (Malayalam, Hindi, Arabic, Urdu, Spanish etc) and an MDC course chosen from a discipline other than Computer Science. CS students cannot choose CS department courses as their MDC.

BSc Computer Science Honours programme under CUFYUGP offers two exit points. The first exit is after Semester 6 which gives a three year UG Degree in Computer Science. Any student can choose this exit without any eligibility condition. The second exit is after Semester 8 which gives a four year UG Degree Honours in Computer Science. Any student who wishes to continue to the fourth year can do so without any eligibility condition except for Honours with Research which requires CGPA 7.5 and above. Within the fourth year there are three pathway options. Regular Honours means the student takes three elective courses in Semester 8 in addition to the Semester 7 courses. Honours with Project means the student does a 12 credit project in Semester 8 instead of three elective courses, open to all fourth year students. Honours with Research means the student does a mandatory 12 credit research project in Semester 8, only available to students with CGPA 7.5 and above cumulatively in first six semesters. The degree awarded for three year exit is Bachelor of Science in Computer Science. The degree awarded for four year completion is Bachelor of Science Honours in Computer Science.

chunks.topic: Minimum Pass Requirements and Failing a Course
chunks.content: The minimum requirement to pass a course is P grade which requires 35% aggregate of internal and external marks combined with a minimum of 30% in external evaluation alone. If a student scores below 35% aggregate or below 30% in external evaluation they receive F grade and fail the course. A student who is absent for the examination receives Ab grade which is also treated as Fail. The successful completion of all courses and capstone components prescribed for the three year or four year programme with minimum P grade is required for the award of the UG Degree or UG Degree Honours or UG Degree Honours with Research. When students take audit courses they are given Pass or Fail grade without any credits. For practical courses students must pass the continuous evaluation component before they are permitted to appear for the end semester practical examination and viva-voce. The process of continuous evaluation of practical courses must be completed before 10 days from the commencement of the end semester examination.

chunks.topic: How to Calculate Target SGPA to Reach a Target CGPA
chunks.content: To find out what SGPA a student needs in remaining semesters to achieve a target CGPA, the following logic applies. CGPA is calculated by dividing the total credit points earned across all semesters by the total credits of all semesters. Total credit points for a semester is SGPA multiplied by total credits of that semester. For the three year programme total credits are 133. For the four year programme total credits are 177. To calculate the required SGPA for remaining semesters: first calculate the total credit points already earned by multiplying each completed semester SGPA by its credits and summing them. Then calculate the total credit points needed for the target CGPA by multiplying target CGPA by total programme credits. Then subtract earned credit points from needed credit points to get the remaining credit points needed. Then divide remaining credit points by remaining credits to get the required average SGPA for remaining semesters. For example if a student has completed Semesters 1 and 2 with SGPAs of 6.5 and 7.0, credits 21 and 21, total credit points earned are 136.5 plus 147 which equals 283.5. If target CGPA is 7.5 for three year programme, total credit points needed are 7.5 multiplied by 133 which equals 997.5. Remaining credit points needed are 997.5 minus 283.5 which equals 714. Remaining credits are 133 minus 42 which equals 91. Required average SGPA is 714 divided by 91 which equals 7.846.

chunks.topic: Minor Course Groups Offered by Computer Science Department
chunks.content: The Computer Science department offers minor courses to students from other departments. CS students cannot take these as their own minor. There are seven minor groups offered by CS department. Group 1 is Foundation of Computer Programming preferable for Physics and Electronics students, consisting of CSC1MN101 Exploring Computer Basics and Computational Thinking in Semester 1, CSC2MN101 Foundations of C Programming in Semester 2, CSC3MN201 Python Programming for Science in Semester 3. Group 2 is Data Science and AI preferable for Mathematics and Data Science students, consisting of CSC1MN102 Python Programming in Semester 1, CSC2MN102 Introduction to Data Science in Semester 2, CSC3MN202 Introduction to Artificial Intelligence in Semester 3. Group 3 is Data Analysis and Visualization preferable for Statistics, Econometrics and Economics students, consisting of CSC1MN103 Data Analysis using Spreadsheet in Semester 1, CSC2MN103 Fundamentals of SPSS and R Programming in Semester 2, CSC3MN203 Data Visualisation using Python in Semester 3. Group 4 is Computing Skills and Programming Fundamentals preferable for Chemistry students, consisting of CSC1MN104 Computer Essentials with Word Processing and Presentation in Semester 1, CSC2MN104 Web Design Trends and Techniques in Semester 2, CSC3MN204 Programming Fundamentals using C in Semester 3. Group 5 is General Computing Principles preferable for Humanities and Commerce students, consisting of CSC1MN105 Introduction to IT in Semester 1, CSC2MN105 Efficient Office Dynamics in Semester 2, CSC3MN205 Mastering Content Management Systems in Semester 3. Group 6 is Fundamentals of Computer Science preferable for Microbiology students, consisting of CSC1MN106 Computer Fundamentals with MS Excel and SPSS in Semester 1, CSC2MN106 Fundamentals of System Software Networks and DBMS in Semester 2, CSC3MN206 Programming using Python in Semester 3. Group 7 is General Awareness in Computer for any stream, consisting of CSC1MN107 Computer Hardware Assembly in Semester 1, CSC2MN107 Exploring Cyber Security in Social Media in Semester 2, CSC3MN207 Emerging Trends in Computer Science in Semester 3. Students who opt minor group 1 should not opt minor group 2 in remaining semesters and vice versa. Students who opt minor group 1 should not opt minor group 4 in remaining semesters and vice versa.

chunks.topic: Vocational Minor Courses Offered by Computer Science Department
chunks.content: The Computer Science department offers Vocational Minor courses to students from other departments under the title Data Science and AI. CS students cannot take these as their own vocational minor. There are two vocational minor groups offered by CS department. Group 1 is Data Science consisting of CSC1VN101 Computational Mathematics in Data Science in Semester 1, CSC2VN101 Fundamentals of Data Science in Semester 2, CSC3VN201 Data Analysis and Visualisation Using Spreadsheets in Semester 3, and CSC8VN301 Predictive Modelling in Semester 8. Group 2 is Artificial Intelligence consisting of CSC1VN102 Statistical Foundations for Artificial Intelligence in Semester 1, CSC2VN102 Foundations of Artificial Intelligence in Semester 2, CSC3VN202 Automation and Robotics in Semester 3, and CSC8VN302 Expert Systems and Fuzzy Logic in Semester 8. Students in Single Major pathway can choose courses from any Minor or Vocational Minor group offered by a discipline other than their Major discipline. Students in Major with Minor pathway can choose all courses from any two Minor groups offered by any discipline. Students in Major with Vocational Minor pathway can choose all courses from any two Vocational Minor groups offered by any discipline. Note that from 2025 admissions the name of CSC2VN101 was changed from Introduction to Data Science to Fundamentals of Data Science.

chunks.topic: Pathway Options for BSc CS Students
chunks.content: BSc Computer Science Honours students under CUFYUGP can choose from three main pathways. Pathway 1 is Single Major where the student focuses only on Computer Science as the major discipline and chooses minor or vocational minor courses from any other discipline. Pathway 2 is Major with Multiple Disciplines where the student takes Computer Science as the primary major and chooses all three courses from any one Minor or Vocational Minor group offered by another discipline as the second discipline. Pathway 3 is Major with Minor where the student takes Computer Science as the major and chooses all courses from any two Minor groups offered by any discipline. If a student chooses two Minor groups from Computer Science department the title of the Minor will be Computer Science. Pathway 4 is Major with Vocational Minor where the student takes Computer Science as the major and chooses all courses from any two Vocational Minor groups offered by any discipline. If a student chooses a Vocational Minor group from Computer Science department the title of the Vocational Minor will be Data Science and AI. Double Major pathway is also available under CUFYUGP but is not commonly offered at all colleges. The pathway choice affects which minor and vocational minor courses the student takes in Semesters 1, 2, and 3 but does not affect the Major courses which remain the same for all CS students regardless of pathway.

The BSc Computer Science Honours programme is implemented under the CUFYUGP Regulations 2024 (Calicut University Four Year Undergraduate Programme). This scheme and syllabus is applicable to students admitted from 2024 onwards. The programme was initially implemented vide U.O.No. 9924/2024/Admn dated 22.06.2024. A modified scheme and syllabus was later approved by the Vice Chancellor and implemented vide U.O.No. 8816/2025/Admn dated 13.06.2025 incorporating corrections pointed out by Pareeksha Bhavan. The Board of Studies in Computer Science and Application (UG) met on 24.05.2025 and recommended the modifications. The modified syllabus is applicable with effect from 2024 admission. Students admitted before 2024 follow a different scheme and syllabus. The FYUGP Navigator app and this information applies only to students admitted in 2024 and later batches under CUFYUGP Regulations 2024. The programme is affiliated to University of Calicut and follows all regulations set by Calicut University.

Semester 1 has 6 courses totaling 21 credits and 525 marks. The fixed Major course is CSC1CJ101 Fundamentals of Computers and Computational Thinking (75 hours, 4 credits). Students choose 2 Minor courses from their chosen minor group from another discipline, each carrying 4 credits. Students take 1 AEC course which is ENG1FA101 English Language Skills for Sciences (60 hours, 3 credits). Students also take 1 Language course of their choice such as Malayalam, Hindi, Arabic, Urdu or Spanish (3 credits). Students take 1 MDC course chosen from a discipline other than Computer Science (3 credits). CS students cannot choose CS department courses as their MDC.

Semester 2 has 6 courses totaling 21 credits and 525 marks. The fixed Major course is CSC2CJ101 Fundamentals of Programming in C Language (75 hours, 4 credits). Students choose 2 Minor courses continuing from their chosen minor group, each carrying 4 credits. Students take 1 AEC course which is ENG2FA103 Advanced English Language Skills for Sciences (60 hours, 3 credits). Students also take 1 Language course of their choice continuing from Semester 1 (3 credits). Students take 1 MDC course chosen from a discipline other than Computer Science (3 credits).

Semester 3 has 6 courses totaling 22 credits and 550 marks. The two fixed Major courses are CSC3CJ201 Software Project Management (60 hours, 4 credits) and CSC3CJ202 Data Structures and Algorithm (75 hours, 4 credits). Students choose 2 Minor courses continuing from their chosen minor group, each carrying 4 credits. Students take 1 VAC course which is ENG3FV108 Framing Values Literary and Cultural Narratives for Sciences (45 hours, 3 credits). Students take 1 MDC course chosen from a discipline other than Computer Science (3 credits). There is no AEC or SEC course in Semester 3.

Semester 4 has 6 courses totaling 21 credits and 525 marks. The three fixed Major courses are CSC4CJ203 Database Management System (75 hours, 4 credits), CSC4CJ204 Fundamentals of Python Programming (75 hours, 4 credits), and CSC4CJ205 Computer Networks (75 hours, 4 credits). Students take 1 VAC course which is ENG4FV109 Framing Perspectives Literary and Cultural Narratives for Sciences (45 hours, 3 credits). Students take 1 SEC course which is ENG4FS111 Contemporary Language Skills for Sciences (60 hours, 3 credits). Students take 1 MDC course chosen from a discipline other than Computer Science (3 credits). There are no Minor courses in Semester 4.

Semester 5 has 6 courses totaling 23 credits and 575 marks. The three fixed Major courses are CSC5CJ301 Data Mining (60 hours, 4 credits), CSC5CJ302 Object Oriented Programming Java (75 hours, 4 credits), and CSC5CJ303 Full Stack Web Development (75 hours, 4 credits). Students choose 2 Elective courses from one specialization group, either Data Science, AI and ML, or Cloud Computing, each carrying 4 credits. Both electives in Semester 5 must be from the same specialization group. The sixth course is SEC Introduction to Digital Marketing (45 hours, 3 credits). There are no VAC, MDC, AEC, or Minor courses in Semester 5. Students must complete their internship before or during Semester 5 as internship evaluation happens at end of Semester 6.

Semester 6 has 6 courses totaling 25 credits and 625 marks. The three fixed Major courses are CSC6CJ304 Digital Electronics and Computer Architecture (60 hours, 4 credits), CSC6CJ305 Principles of Operating Systems (75 hours, 4 credits), and CSC6CJ306 Introduction to Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning (75 hours, 4 credits). Students choose 2 Elective courses from the same specialization group they chose in Semester 5, each carrying 4 credits. The sixth course is SEC Project Implementation (45 hours, 3 credits). The 2 credit Internship is also evaluated and awarded at the end of Semester 6 making the total 25 credits. Students who do not wish to continue to the fourth year exit after Semester 6 with a three year UG degree.

Semester 7 has 5 courses totaling 20 credits and 500 marks. All five courses are fixed Major courses: CSC7CJ401 Theory of Computation (60 hours, 4 credits), CSC7CJ402 System Security (60 hours, 4 credits), CSC7CJ403 Advanced Data Structures and Algorithms (75 hours, 4 credits), CSC7CJ404 Blockchain Technology (60 hours, 4 credits), and CSC7CJ405 Internet of Things (75 hours, 4 credits). There are no electives, foundation courses, or minor courses in Semester 7. Semester 7 is only for students who chose to continue to the fourth year Honours programme.

Semester 8 has different course structures depending on the Honours pathway chosen. For Regular Honours students take 3 fixed Major courses which are CSC8CJ406 Compiler Design (60 hours, 4 credits), CSC8CJ407 Client Server Architecture (60 hours, 4 credits), and CSC8CJ408 Parallel Computing (60 hours, 4 credits), plus 3 Elective courses from the basket without specialization restriction, totaling 24 credits. For Honours with Project students replace the 3 Major courses with a 12 credit Project and take 3 Elective courses, totaling 24 credits. For Honours with Research students do a mandatory 12 credit Research Project and take 2 Elective courses or replace one elective with Research Methodology CSC8CJ, totaling 24 credits. Elective courses available in Semester 8 without specialization include Microprocessor and its Applications, System Software, Social Network Analysis, Advanced Distributed Computing, Cyber Forensic, Ethical Hacking, and Expert Systems.
